claims.text: We work in (1+1)-dimensional Minkowski spacetime with metric signature (+,-).
ds^2 = dt^2 - dx^2
An observer undergoing constant proper acceleration a has a worldline parametrized by proper time τ.
The four-velocity u^μ = (dt/dτ, dx/dτ) satisfies u^μ u_μ = (dt/dτ)^2 - (dx/dτ)^2 = 1.
The four-acceleration a^μ = du^μ/dτ must satisfy a^μ a_μ = -a^2.
Orthogonality u^μ a_μ = 0 holds.
Assuming motion along the x-axis and that the observer is momentarily at rest at t=0 with x = 1/a.
From orthogonality, (dt/dτ)(d²t/dτ²) - (dx/dτ)(d²x/dτ²) = 0.
Using the constant proper acceleration condition, one finds d/dt(v/√(1-v²)) = a, where v = dx/dt.
Integrating with v=0 at t=0 gives v/√(1-v²) = at.
v = at/√(1+a²t²)
γ = dt/dτ = √(1+a²t²)
Integrating dx/dt = v with x(0)=1/a yields x(t) = (1/a)√(1+a²t²).
The worldline is a hyperbola: x² - t² = 1/a².
We introduce coordinates (η, ξ) that cover the right Rindler wedge x > |t|.
The coordinate transformation is t = ξ sinh η, x = ξ cosh η, with ξ > 0, -∞ < η < ∞.
x² - t² = ξ²(cosh²η - sinh²η) = ξ².
The observer's hyperbola x² - t² = 1/a² corresponds to ξ = 1/a = constant.
The proper time along this worldline is given by dτ = ξ dη = dη/a, so η = aτ (up to an additive constant).
dt = sinh η dξ + ξ cosh η dη
dx = cosh η dξ + ξ sinh η dη
ds² = dt² - dx² = (sinh²η - cosh²η)dξ² + 2ξ(sinhη coshη - coshη sinhη)dξdη + ξ²(cosh²η - sinh²η)dη²
ds² = -dξ² + ξ²dη²
An observer at constant ξ has proper acceleration α = 1/ξ.
claims.notes: Stated without explicit derivation from the metric.

claims.text: For ξ = 1/a, we recover α = a.
The lines t = ±x (with x>0) bound the right Rindler wedge.
In Rindler coordinates, these correspond to ξ = 0 and η → ±∞.
As ξ → 0 with finite η, we approach the origin (t,x)=(0,0), but the lines t = ±x are reached by taking η → ±∞ while ξ → 0 such that t/x = tanh η → ±1.
The metric component g_{ηη} = ξ² vanishes at ξ=0, making it a coordinate singularity.
In Minkowski coordinates, along t = ±x, we have ds² = 0, so these are null surfaces.
They constitute the Rindler horizon, a Killing horizon associated with the boost Killing vector ∂_η.
Consider a free massless scalar field φ in (1+1)-dimensional Minkowski spacetime with field equation ∂_t²φ - ∂_x²φ = 0.
We use units where ℏ = c = k_B = 1.
The field can be expanded in plane-wave modes: φ = ∫dk (a_k u_k + a_k† u_k*).
The positive-frequency modes (with respect to Minkowski time t) are u_k = (1/√(4π|k|)) e^{-i|k|t + ikx}.
These satisfy (u_k, u_{k'}) = δ(k-k') with the Klein-Gordon inner product.
The Klein-Gordon inner product is (φ₁, φ₂) = i ∫dx (φ₁* ∂_t φ₂ - (∂_t φ₁*) φ₂).
The Minkowski vacuum |0_M⟩ is defined by a_k |0_M⟩ = 0 for all k.
In the right Rindler wedge, we use coordinates (η, ξ) with metric ds² = ξ²dη² - dξ².
The field equation in Rindler coordinates becomes ∂_η²φ = ξ²∂_ξ²φ + ξ∂_ξφ.
Define light-cone coordinates U = t - x, V = t + x.
In the right wedge, U < 0, V > 0.
The transformation to Rindler coordinates gives U = -ξ e^{-η}, V = ξ e^{η}.
A right-moving mode depends only on U.
A positive-frequency Rindler mode in the right wedge is v_Ω^{(R)} = N_Ω θ(-U)(-U)^{iΩ} = N_Ω θ(-U) e^{iΩ ln(-U)}.
When expressed in (η,ξ), this becomes v_Ω^{(R)} = N_Ω e^{-iΩη} ξ^{iΩ} (up to a phase).
For the left wedge (where U>0, V<0), we define positive-frequency modes v_Ω^{(L)} = N_Ω θ(V) V^{-iΩ}.
claims.notes: Left wedge has V<0 but mode uses θ(V); possibly an error.

claims.text: The field expansion in terms of Rindler modes is φ = ∫₀^∞ dΩ (b_Ω^{(R)} v_Ω^{(R)} + b_Ω^{(R)†} v_Ω^{(R)*} + b_Ω^{(L)} v_Ω^{(L)} + b_Ω^{(L)†} v_Ω^{(L)*}).
The Minkowski and Rindler mode expansions represent the same field, so the operators are related by a Bogoliubov transformation.
The Bogoliubov coefficients are computed by taking inner products: α_{ωΩ} = (v_Ω^{(R)}, u_ω), β_{ωΩ} = -(v_Ω^{(R)*}, u_ω).

Because e^{-iωU} is analytic in the lower half of the complex U-plane (since ω>0), and the Rindler modes have specific analyticity properties, the Bogoliubov coefficients can be computed via integrals.
After careful calculation, one finds α_{ωΩ} = (1/√(2πaω)) e^{πΩ/(2a)} (ω/a)^{iΩ/a} Γ(-iΩ/a).
claims.notes: Invoked from Birrell & Davies / Crispino et al.
Invoked from Birrell & Davies / Crispino et al.

claims.text: The Minkowski vacuum can be expressed as an entangled state of Rindler particles: |0_M⟩ = ∏_Ω (1/√(1-e^{-2πΩ/a})) exp(e^{-πΩ/a} b_Ω^{(R)†} b_Ω^{(L)†}) |0_R⟩ ⊗ |0_L⟩.
claims.notes: Stated as a result of two-mode squeezing structure.

claims.text: Tracing over the left wedge degrees of freedom yields a thermal density matrix for the right wedge: ρ_R = ∏_Ω (1-e^{-2πΩ/a}) Σ_{n=0}^∞ e^{-2πnΩ/a} |n_Ω⟩⟨n_Ω|.
This is a thermal Bose–Einstein distribution with temperature T = a/(2π) in natural units.
Restoring constants, the Unruh temperature is T = ℏa/(2πck_B).
The derivation relies on the Bogoliubov transformation between Minkowski and Rindler mode decompositions.
The essential physical assumption is that the Minkowski vacuum state is the physically relevant state for inertial observers.
Quantum field theory in curved spacetime can be constructed by defining positive-frequency modes with respect to the appropriate time coordinate (here, Rindler time η).
The thermal spectrum emerges because the notions of positive frequency differ between inertial and accelerating observers, leading to particle production in the accelerated frame.
